content: The Constitution of Nepal provides a robust framework for these rights. Under Part 3 (Articles 16 to 45), 31 fundamental rights are guaranteed, with Article 46 providing for constitutional remedies if these rights are violated. Key protections include: Article 17 (Right to Freedom, including peaceful assembly without arms), Article 18 (Right to Equality), Article 20 (Rights relating to Justice), Article 27 (Right to Information), and Article 16 (Right to live with Dignity).

Furthermore, the National Human Rights Commission Act (2012), the Civil Rights Act (1955), and various child and prison acts support these protections. Nepal is also a party to 18 international human rights treaties. Under Section 9 of the Nepal Treaty Act, 1990, treaty provisions prevail if they conflict with national laws, effectively making international human rights standards part of Nepal's domestic legal landscape.

There is no dispute that the 'Gen Z' generation organized the protest on September 8, 2025, against corruption and the social media ban, exercising their fundamental right to peaceful assembly under Article 17(2)(b) of the Constitution.

Preliminary evaluation of reports from the National Human Rights Commission suggests that state forces committed excesses against mostly unarmed protesters. Security forces used widespread gunfire and baton charges, resulting in significant casualties.
Casualty and Incident Summary

A total of 37 protesters died in the Kathmandu Valley alone. On September 8, 17 individuals were killed outside the Parliament premises, and 2 others were killed outside the capital. Twelve others who were injured that day succumbed to their wounds later. In total, 1,084 protesters were injured by gunfire or other means. On the side of the security forces, 1,618 police personnel were injured, and 3 were brutally killed by protesters.

Security forces expended 7,873 rounds of ammunition, including 1,081 rounds from SLR semi-automatic weapons. Autopsy reports confirm deaths were caused by fatal shots to the head, chest, and abdomen. Victims included teenagers from the Gen Z generation. Analysis of audio-visual materials indicates that extreme suppression occurred under the guise of enforcing curfews and prohibited zones.

The Commission notes that accountability for state actions rests with the highest level of policy-makers and executive leadership. While former PM K.P. Sharma Oli and former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak claimed they did not order the shooting and were unaware of it, CDO Chhabiraj Rijal testified that the Home Minister had instructed him to use 'force in a sequence' to control the situation.
Legal Framework for Accountability: Recklessness and Negligence

The Commission finds that the actions of certain high-level officials attract charges under the National Penal Code, 2017 (2074 BS). Specifically, their behavior is categorized as 'Reckless' (Section 181) and 'Negligent' (Section 182).

Recklessness (लापरबाही) is defined as acting with total disregard for the dangerous consequences or risks to the life and safety of others. Judicial precedents, such as *Duckers v. Lynch*, describe it as an indifference to the results of one's actions.

Negligence (हेलचेक्र्याई) refers to the failure to exercise the care that a reasonably prudent person would show in similar circumstances. Per *Jacob Mathew v. State of Punjab*, three components are required: 1) A legal duty of care, 2) A breach of that duty, and 3) Consequential damage or death.

Based on the gravity of their involvement and the failure to perform their official duties during the four-hour shooting outside the Parliament, the Commission recommends legal action and prosecution against the following individuals:

content.content: Then-Prime Minister Mr. K.P. Sharma Oli
Then-Home Minister Mr. Ramesh Lekhak
Then-Inspector General of Police Mr. Chandra Kuber Khapung

content: The responsibility lies with the authorities to ensure that the use of ultimate force (firing metal bullets) in anti-government or any protests is adopted only as a last resort.

There was a deliberate violation of duty. Evidence collected by the Commission shows that despite hours of live firing, no efforts were made to stop it, reflecting a failure to adhere to the principles of Proportionality and Necessity. No 'Corrective Orders' were issued to halt the firing of metal bullets. Theoretically, it is unacceptable for high-ranking officials to evade responsibility by claiming they were unaware of the 17 deaths outside the Parliament premises on September 8. Even in peaceful processions organized under the constitutional guarantee of safety and the right to unarmed assembly, excessive force was deployed.

Nepal operates under a parliamentary system, which is a prime ministerial system. It was a significant failure on the part of the Prime Minister, as the guardian of all citizens, to not intervene and prevent further human loss while shooting continued for nearly four hours outside the Parliament, resulting in several deaths and hundreds of injuries.

During the inquiry, then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli did not answer all questions related to the incident. He was uncooperative, stating that the questions were more suited for SEE (secondary level) students or field-level police officers.

Then-Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak and Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli failed to take initiatives to prevent high casualties on the afternoon of September 8. Although a meeting was called at 2:00 PM on that day, the National Security Council meeting only took place after 10:00 PM, by which time significant loss of life had already occurred at 1:00 PM, and nearby hospitals were filled with hundreds of gunshot victims.

Based on the analysis, while there may not have been a prior intent to commit murder, the negligence and failure of the security forces to stop firing after the initial casualties led to the deaths of several individuals, including teenagers.
Therefore, the leadership is held responsible for the deaths of these individuals due to recklessness and negligence during the September 8 incident.

On September 8, unlike the events of September 9, there were no instances of protesters attacking police, snatching weapons, or setting the Parliament building on fire. Protesters in public areas had thrown bricks and stones and set fire to a room near the south gate, but they had not advanced to burn the building. Security forces should have adopted alternative procedures. No steps were taken to maintain peace through other means once the firing and casualties began.

As a result, 17 people died that day, and 12 others injured at the site died later. The events of September 8 triggered the violence of September 9, leading to a total of 76 deaths. The burning of the country, looting, and high death toll were direct consequences of the government's failure to stop the firing on the 8th and its failure to take preventive security measures for the 9th. The reckless and negligent behavior of the Prime Minister and other officials in not stopping the gunfire led to excessive human loss.

Regarding the deaths caused by police fire outside the Baneshwor Parliament premises on September 8, Section 181(1) of the Penal Code, 2017 (2074 BS) prohibits killing anyone through a reckless act. Section 181(2) stipulates that such acts, if not covered under Sections 177 (intent), 178 (knowledge of probable death), or 179 (provocation), constitute a crime. The three mentioned high-ranking officials showed 'Culpable Negligence' under Section 181 and 'Negligence' under Section 182 by allowing the shooting to continue for four hours.

Consequently, it has been decided to recommend that the Government of Nepal investigate and prosecute former Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli, former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak, and former IGP Chandra Kuber Khapung under Section 181(1) and Section 182 of the Penal Code, 2017.
Recommendation for Action Against Home Secretary and Three Others under Section 182

The roles, lapses, and negligence of political, administrative, and security heads regarding the September 8 Parliament premises incident have been discussed. The following four officials must be held accountable for their specific roles and responsibilities.

content.content: Then-Home Secretary Mr. Gokarna Mani Duwadi
Inspector General of Armed Police Force Mr. Raju Aryal
Then-Chief of National Investigation Department Mr. Hutraj Thapa
Then-Chief District Officer of Kathmandu Mr. Chhabilal Rijal

content: These officials are found to have been involved in the negligence that led to the deaths. They remained indifferent to using their administrative authority to stop the four-hour clash and indirectly supported the use of force. They failed to take any initiative to restore peace even after the first deaths occurred.

The investigation shows that the Executive Head, the Home Minister, and these officials failed to exercise the necessary caution, thereby consenting to and assisting in the loss of life through negligence. This falls under the crime of 'Negligence' as defined by Section 182 of the Penal Code, 2017.

Therefore, the Commission recommends the Government of Nepal to investigate and prosecute Mr. Gokarna Mani Duwadi, Mr. Raju Aryal, Mr. Hutraj Thapa, and Mr. Chhabilal Rijal under Section 182(1) of the Penal Code, 2017, for the reckless and negligent acts that resulted in the deaths of several people, including teenagers.
Regarding the current IGP Mr. Dan Bahadur Karki (then AIG at the Kathmandu Valley Police Office during the September 9 incident):

On September 8, he was an AIG at the Human Resource Development Department. Following that day's events, the Cabinet transferred him to the vacant AIG post at the Valley Police Office. He assumed duty at 9:00 AM on September 9. He was then called to the Ministry of Home Affairs and spent the day with the IGP and other security heads, eventually moving to the Army Headquarters.

When the movement turned violent and police circles were attacked, he issued orders via his 'Victor Control' set to prepare lists of detainees and release them if necessary to ensure the safety of police personnel, weapons, and vehicles, and instructed using no force other than tear gas. This was not a 'blanket' order. He also facilitated the helicopter rescue of police officers in Maharajgunj after three personnel were brutally killed. Since he acted under the direct instructions of IGP Chandra Kuber Khapung while stationed at Army HQ, he cannot be held solely responsible for these orders. However, he is cited for failing to ensure that weaponry was properly secured or decommissioned before falling into the hands of criminals.
For these lapses, the Commission recommends the Government of Nepal to take departmental action against IGP Mr. Dan Bahadur Karki under Rule 109 of the Police Rules, 2014 (2071 BS).
Recommendations for Action: Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, and NID

The following officials of the Nepal Police are recommended for disciplinary action under Section 9(4) of the Police Act, 1955 and Rule 109 of the Police Rules, 2014 for their failure to perform their duties on September 8 and 9:

content.content: AIG Mr. Siddhi Bikram Shah, Operations Head, Police HQ
DIG Mr. Om Bahadur Rana, Then-Acting Chief of Valley Police
DIG Mr. Bishwa Adhikari, Then-Chief of Kathmandu District Police
SSP Mr. Deep Shamsher J.B.R., Valley Police Office
SP Mr. Rishiiram Kandel, Special Task Force, Parliament House

content: Recommendations for Action: Armed Police Force

content.content: Then-Operation Commander AIG Mr. Narayan Datta Paudel, APF HQ
DIG Mr. Suresh Kumar Shrestha, Valley Commander, APF
SP Mr. Jiban KC, Disaster Rescue, Sinamangal

content: Action against these APF officers is recommended under Rule 112 of the Armed Police Force Rules, 2015 (2072 BS).
Recognition for Protecting Weapons and Stations

The Commission recommends that the Ministry of Home Affairs identify and reward those police officers and civilians who risked their lives to defend police headquarters, circles (including Naxal, Halchowk, and Bhadrakali), and weaponry from violent attackers on September 9.
National Investigation Department (NID)

It has been observed that the National Investigation Department failed to provide accurate and timely intelligence regarding the events of September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082), as discussed in the previous analysis. Therefore, the following officials are recommended for disciplinary action:

content.content: Mr. Krishna Prasad Khanal, Co-Director, Valley Campaign Division
Mr. Riben Kumar Gachhadar, Co-Investigation Director and Kathmandu District Chief

content: The Commission recommends that the Government of Nepal initiate proceedings against these individuals in accordance with Rule 10.2 of the Nepal Special Service Rules, 1987 (2044 BS), including the 19th amendment, and the Nepal Special Service Act, 1985 (2042 BS).
Recommendations for Action Against Army Personnel and the TOB Group Regarding Security Lapses at Vital Installations
The four vital locations where the Nepal Army was deployed for security:

content.content: Presidential Palace (Shital Niwas): Security team led by Brigadier General Mr. Manoj Baidawar.
Prime Minister’s Residence (Baluwatar): Security team led by Lieutenant Colonel Mr. Diwakar Khadka.
Singha Durbar Secretariat: Security team led by Lieutenant Colonel Mr. Ganesh Khadka (also a member of the District Security Committee).
Parliament House/International Convention Center (Baneswor): Security team led by Major Mr. Santosh Dhungel.
